body:
## Summary

`FsChunkStore::put_fragment` writes to a temp file whose name is keyed **only** on the `FragmentId`, then atomically renames it onto the final path. Because the temp path is not unique per call, **two concurrent writes of the same `FragmentId` race on the same `.tmp` file**. The fix is not a lock — it is a unique temp file per write, letting the atomic `rename` be the serialization point.

## Root cause

`temp_path` is keyed on the id alone (`crates/chunkstore-fs/src/lib.rs:45-49`):

```rust
fn temp_path(&self, id: FragmentId) -> PathBuf {
    self.root
        .join(format!("{:032x}", id.chunk))
        .join(format!("{:05}.tmp", id.index))   // shared per FragmentId — not unique per call
}
```

and `put_fragment` does verify → write temp → rename (`crates/chunkstore-fs/src/lib.rs:71-85`):

```rust
Self::verify(id, fragment.as_ref())?;           // verifies the INPUT bytes
let final_path = self.fragment_path(id);
// create_dir_all(chunk_dir) ...
let temp = self.temp_path(id);
fs::write(&temp, &fragment)?;                    // two writers → same temp
fs::rename(&temp, final_path)?;                  // second rename can hit NotFound
```

There is no per-fragment synchronization in the store. Concurrent writes of **different** fragments are safe (distinct paths); concurrent writes of the **same** `FragmentId` share scratch space and race.

## How it is reached

The D server accepts arbitrary concurrent writes: `DServer::serve` runs a tonic server over HTTP/2, the store is held as `Arc<S>` and each `PutFragment` runs on its own task (`crates/chunkstore-grpc/src/server.rs:33-36`, `:46-60`). Nothing serializes writes at the gRPC/service layer.

A single chunk fans **one** fragment to each of `n` *distinct* D servers (`FanoutChunkStore::route(index) = stores[index % n]`, `crates/chunkstore-grpc/src/fanout.rs:58-59`), so the same-id collision is not from one chunk write. It arises from:

- **idempotent client retries** of a `PutFragment`;
- **repair / reconstruction** writing a fragment that a foreground write (or another repair pass) is also writing — and in the single-binary profile the gateway and custodian can share the store in-process (`ChunkStoreService::from_arc`).

## Impact / severity

Mitigated, not corrupting:

- the same `FragmentId` carries the same logical (deterministic EC) bytes, each already checksum-verified on input (`lib.rs:74`);
- the final publish is an atomic `rename`, so the file under `<index>.frag` is always complete;
- `get_fragment` re-verifies the checksum on read (`crates/chunkstore-fs/src/lib.rs:87-97`), so any anomaly surfaces as a read error → repair, never silent bad data.

But it is a real robustness corner: interleaved `fs::write`s can clobber the shared temp, and the second `rename` can fail `NotFound` and **spuriously error a concurrent duplicate/repair write**. Latent, not steady-state.

## Fix — unique temp + atomic rename (not a lock)

A per-fragment mutex map would only serialize **within one process**, adds no crash-safety the rename doesn't already give, and is more machinery (async lock map + eviction). You don't need mutual exclusion — you need *private scratch + atomic publish*. Give each write its own temp:

```rust
fn temp_path(&self, id: FragmentId) -> PathBuf {
    static SEQ: AtomicU64 = AtomicU64::new(0);
    let uniq = SEQ.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
    self.root
        .join(format!("{:032x}", id.chunk))
        .join(format!("{:05}.{}.{}.tmp", id.index, std::process::id(), uniq))
}
```

- each writer writes to its own temp — no interleaved clobbering;
- `rename(own_temp, final)` is atomic and replaces — concurrent renames onto `<index>.frag` always leave a complete, verified file; the loser's inode is unlinked; no `NotFound`;
- last-writer-wins is a semantic no-op (same id ⇒ same bytes);
- correct across processes too (pid component), strictly better than an in-process lock.

`list_fragments` is unaffected: it accepts only names ending exactly `.frag` (`parse_fragment_file_name`, `crates/chunkstore-fs/src/lib.rs:221-223`), so the unique `.tmp` names stay skipped.

Tidier alternative: the `tempfile` crate (already a workspace dev-dependency) — `NamedTempFile::new_in(chunk_dir)?` (O_EXCL unique temp) + `.persist(final_path)?` (atomic rename); would need promoting to a real dependency of `chunkstore-fs`.

## Consequence to handle

The current stable temp name is self-cleaning (the next write to the same id overwrites it), so a crash leaves **at most one** `<index>.tmp` per fragment. Unique temps instead leave `<index>.<pid>.<seq>.tmp` **litter** after a crash. Reap stale `.tmp` files older than a grace window — natural home is the **GC/scrub custodian loop** (it already walks the tree for orphans); a simpler stopgap is to sweep on `FsChunkStore::open`.

## Definition of done

- [ ] `put_fragment` uses a unique temp path per call; concurrent writes of the **same** `FragmentId` no longer race
- [ ] regression test: N concurrent `put_fragment` calls for the same fragment all succeed (no spurious error) and the final file verifies
- [ ] `list_fragments` still ignores temp files
- [ ] stale temp files are reaped (GC/scrub loop, or on `open`)

## Out of scope (adjacent, flagged not bundled)

The write path does not `fsync` the temp before the rename nor the parent directory after, so an acknowledged fragment can be lost on power failure even once this concurrency fix lands. The robust idiom is write → `fsync(file)` → `rename` → `fsync(dir)`. This is a **durability** concern, separate from the concurrency bug above — file separately if wanted.

## Refs

- `crates/chunkstore-fs/src/lib.rs` — `temp_path:45-49`, `put_fragment:71-85`, `get_fragment:87-97`, `parse_fragment_file_name:221-223`
- `crates/chunkstore-grpc/src/server.rs:33-36,46-60` (concurrent `PutFragment` over `Arc<store>`); `crates/chunkstore-grpc/src/fanout.rs:58-59` (one fragment per distinct server)
- ADR-0032 (D-server on-disk fragment layout) — `FsChunkStore` is the dev/NAS **and** interim production networked-D-server backend

milestone.title: M3 — Custodians
milestone.description: The self-maintaining durability plane (L4): GC, scrub, reconstruction, rebalance + durability telemetry. Makes single-zone trustworthy; gates the Step-2 release. Plan: proposal 0005.

title: FsChunkStore: concurrent writes of the same fragment id race on a shared temp path — use a unique temp per write
